title: Pocket Aces on a Dry Board
actionHistory: You raised preflop from BTN, BB called. Flop is K-7-2 rainbow. BB checks to you.

options.optionExplanation: Checking with the best hand on a dry board misses value. Your opponent will often have a king or a draw they'll pay off.
Betting about half pot is perfect. You extract value from kings, middle pairs, and draws while keeping weaker hands in.
Full pot is too large on this dry board. It folds out the hands you want to call and only gets action from hands that beat you or have strong draws.

explanation: Pocket aces on a K-7-2 rainbow board is an extremely strong hand. The board is dry, meaning there are few draws and your overpair is very likely the best hand. The key concept here is that you should bet for value when you're ahead — and on this board, you're almost always ahead. A half-pot bet is ideal because it extracts value from Kx hands, pocket pairs, and even ace-high hands that might float. Checking is a significant mistake because you miss value on one of the safest boards possible for your hand.
keyConceptSummary: On dry boards with an overpair, bet for value. Don't slow-play when there's money to be made.
villainRange: BB calling range: Kx, 77, 22, suited connectors, broadways

title: Pocket Aces on a Dangerous Board
actionHistory: You 3-bet preflop from BTN, MP called. Flop is J-T-9 with two hearts. Villain checks.

options.optionExplanation: Checking here is dangerous. This board connects with many hands in villain's range, and giving a free card could be costly.
A roughly 2/3 pot bet protects your hand against the many draws on this board while still extracting value from top pair and middle pair.
A full pot bet is acceptable but slightly too large. It gets called mostly by hands that have good equity against you.

explanation: This is a critical hand strength concept: the same pocket aces that were a monster on K-7-2 are in a much more vulnerable spot on J-T-9 with two hearts. This board connects heavily with your opponent's calling range — they can have QJ, JT, T9, Q8, KQ, flush draws, and even made straights with KQ or Q8. Your aces are still likely ahead, but they need protection. Betting 2/3 pot charges draws the maximum they'll pay while still getting calls from one-pair hands. This illustrates why hand strength is contextual — the same hand can require very different actions depending on the board texture.
keyConceptSummary: Hand strength is contextual. Aces on a wet, connected board need protection through sizing, unlike on a dry board.
villainRange: MP calling 3-bet range: JJ-99, AJs, ATs, KQs, QJs, JTs, T9s, suited hearts

title: Top Pair Good Kicker on the Flop
actionHistory: You raised from CO, BB called. Flop is K-8-3 rainbow. BB checks.

options.optionExplanation: You have a very strong hand with top pair top kicker. Checking misses value from worse kings and middle pairs.
A small bet of about half pot is ideal on this dry board. It gets calls from Kx, pocket pairs, and even some ace-high hands.
A full pot bet is too large for a dry board. You want to keep weaker hands in the pot.

explanation: Ace-King on a K-8-3 rainbow board gives you top pair with the best possible kicker. This is a very strong hand — you beat all other Kx hands except the unlikely KK. On a dry board with no flush draws and no straight draws, a small bet is optimal because you want to extract value from a wide range of hands. Bigger bets fold out the weaker holdings you want to keep around. The key lesson: when you have a strong made hand on a safe board, bet for value but size it to keep your opponent's calling range wide.
keyConceptSummary: Top pair top kicker on a dry board is very strong. Use small bets to extract value from a wide range of worse hands.
villainRange: BB calling range: Kx, 88, 33, suited connectors, broadways

title: Middle Pair Facing a Bet
actionHistory: BTN raised preflop, you called from BB. Flop is K-9-3 rainbow. You checked, BTN bets 30.

options.optionExplanation: Folding middle pair with a backdoor flush draw is too tight. You have decent equity and a good price.
Calling is correct. You have middle pair which is often good, a backdoor flush draw, and the pot odds to continue.
Raising with middle pair is aggressive without enough value. You'd only get called by better hands.

explanation: Middle pair on a K-9-3 board is a marginal but playable hand. The button's continuation bet doesn't necessarily mean they have a king — they'll c-bet this board with most of their range. You have a pair of nines which beats all unpaired hands, and you have backdoor heart possibilities. The pot is offering you good odds to call. The mistake many beginners make is either folding every time they don't have top pair (too tight) or raising with middle pair (turning a showdown-value hand into a bluff). Calling and re-evaluating on the turn is the balanced, correct play.
keyConceptSummary: Middle pair is a call, not a fold and not a raise. Don't overreact to a single bet — evaluate your hand strength relative to the action.
villainRange: BTN c-bet range: overpairs, Kx, broadways, suited connectors, air

title: Flush Draw on the Flop — Know Your Equity
actionHistory: You raised BTN, BB called. Flop K-7-2 with two hearts. BB checks to you.

options.optionExplanation: Checking isn't terrible but misses the chance to build the pot with a hand that has great equity. You also have fold equity if villain has air.
Betting is excellent. You have the nut flush draw (35% equity to improve), two overcards, and fold equity. This is a textbook semi-bluff.
A larger bet works but is riskier. The half-pot bet achieves the same goals with less risk.

explanation: The nut flush draw gives you approximately 35% equity to make your flush by the river, plus your ace can pair for top pair. This hand illustrates a key concept: draws have real equity and should often be played aggressively. Betting here serves multiple purposes — you might win immediately if villain folds (fold equity), you build the pot for when you hit, and you maintain the initiative. This is called a semi-bluff: you're betting with a hand that isn't the best right now but has excellent chances to improve. Understanding your draw equity and combining it with fold equity is fundamental to good poker.
keyConceptSummary: Strong draws like the nut flush draw have significant equity. Bet them as semi-bluffs to combine fold equity with draw equity.
villainRange: BB calling range: Kx, pocket pairs, suited connectors

title: Two Pair on a Wet Board
actionHistory: CO raised, you called from BB. Flop is J-T-6 rainbow. Action is on you.

options.optionExplanation: Check-calling is okay but with two pair on a board that could develop draws on the turn, leading for value is stronger.
Leading with a donk bet here is justified because you have a very strong hand and want to build the pot before scare cards arrive.
A pot-sized bet is acceptable but may fold out some hands you'd like to keep in.

explanation: Two pair is a very strong hand that needs to build the pot quickly. On a J-T-6 board, there are potential straight draws (KQ, Q9, 98) and the board could get scary on the turn with many overcards or completing draws. Leading out for value (sometimes called a donk bet) makes sense here because you want to start building the pot while your two pair is almost certainly the best hand. If you check and the turn brings a queen, king, or nine, your hand becomes much harder to play. The concept: with strong but vulnerable hands, prioritize getting value now rather than trapping.
keyConceptSummary: Two pair on a board with potential draws should be played aggressively. Build the pot before the board gets dangerous.
villainRange: CO range: overpairs, top pair, broadways, suited connectors, draws

title: Set on the Flop — Maximizing Value
actionHistory: You raised BTN, BB called. Flop is K-8-3 rainbow. BB checks.

options.optionExplanation: A small bet is perfect. On this dry board your set is in no danger. Bet small to keep villain's wide range in the pot — they'll call with Kx, pairs, and even ace-high.
Checking to slow-play is tempting but unnecessary. On such a dry board, you're unlikely to get two streets of value if you check the flop.
A full pot bet is too large. It narrows villain's calling range to only strong hands, reducing your overall value.

explanation: Flopping a set is one of the strongest hands in Hold'em — you have three eights, and on a dry K-8-3 board there's almost nothing that beats you. The mistake beginners make is slow-playing here by checking. While it feels clever to trap, the math says otherwise: on a dry board, the turn and river are unlikely to improve your opponent's hand, so you need to bet on every street to maximize value. A small bet keeps the widest range of hands calling — any king, any pocket pair, even ace-high might call a small bet. The goal with sets is to extract maximum value over multiple streets, not to spring a dramatic trap.
keyConceptSummary: With a set on a dry board, bet small for value on every street. Don't slow-play — build the pot steadily.
villainRange: BB defense range: Kx, pocket pairs, suited connectors, broadways

title: Ace High on a Low Board
actionHistory: You raised BTN, BB called. Flop is 7-5-3 rainbow. BB checks.

options.optionExplanation: C-betting small with ace-high on a low board is standard. You'll win the pot immediately much of the time, and if called, you still have six outs to top pair.
Checking gives up the chance to win the pot with the best hand. Your ace-high often has the most equity here.
A large bet with just ace-high risks too much. If called, you're likely behind.

explanation: Ace-Queen on a 7-5-3 board has no pair, but it's often still the best hand. Your opponent will have missed this board frequently — hands like KJ, QT, J9 have nothing here. A small c-bet takes the pot down a high percentage of the time. When called, you still have overcards that can improve. This hand teaches an important concept: hand strength isn't just about made hands. Ace-high has real showdown value and real fold equity, making it a profitable bet on boards that don't connect with your opponent's range.
keyConceptSummary: Ace-high on a low, dry board has more value than beginners think. A small c-bet wins the pot frequently.
villainRange: BB defense range: pocket pairs, suited connectors, broadways, suited aces

title: Straight Draw vs. Made Hand Decision
actionHistory: CO raised, you called from BB. Flop is T-7-2 rainbow. You checked, CO bets 35.

options.optionExplanation: Folding an open-ended straight draw getting good pot odds is a significant leak. You have 8 outs to the nuts.
Calling is correct. You have an open-ended straight draw with 8 outs, giving you about 32% equity. The pot is offering good odds.
Check-raising as a semi-bluff has merit but is high-variance. Against a tight range, calling is safer and more profitable.

explanation: With 9-8 suited on a T-7-2 board, you have an open-ended straight draw — any jack or any six makes the nuts. That's 8 outs, which gives you approximately 32% equity to hit by the river using the rule of four (8 outs x 4). You need to call 35 into a pot of 95 (60 + 35), needing about 27% equity. Since you have 32%, this is a clearly profitable call. Understanding your draw's equity and comparing it to the price you're getting is the foundation of good poker math.
keyConceptSummary: An open-ended straight draw has 8 outs and roughly 32% equity. Always compare your equity to the pot odds before deciding.
villainRange: CO c-bet range: overpairs, top pair, broadways, air

title: Bottom Pair — When to Let Go
actionHistory: BTN raised, you called from BB. Flop is A-K-5 rainbow. You checked, BTN bets 35.

options.optionExplanation: Folding is correct. Bottom pair on an A-K-5 board is nearly worthless — villain's range is loaded with aces and kings.
Calling with bottom pair against a range full of Ax and Kx is burning money. Even if villain is bluffing, they have many outs.
Raising with bottom pair as a bluff on an A-K board is suicide. Villain is never folding an ace or king.

explanation: This scenario teaches a crucial lesson: not all pairs are created equal. Bottom pair of fives on an A-K-5 board is essentially worthless. Your opponent raised preflop from the button and bet the flop — their range is heavily weighted toward aces, kings, and AK. Your pair of fives only beats complete air, and even bluffs often have overcards that dominate you. Learning to fold weak hands when the board heavily favors your opponent's range is one of the biggest money-savers in poker. Don't fall in love with a pair just because you connected with the board.
keyConceptSummary: Bottom pair on a board with two overcards from your opponent's range is nearly worthless. Learn to fold and save chips.
villainRange: BTN raising and betting range: Ax, Kx, broadways, pocket pairs

title: Overpair on the Turn Facing Aggression
actionHistory: You raised UTG, BTN called. You c-bet flop J-8-4, BTN called. Turn is a second Jack. You checked, BTN bets 80.

options.optionExplanation: Calling is correct. Your overpair beats all bluffs, and the paired board actually reduces the chance villain has a jack. You're getting decent odds.
Folding an overpair on a paired board is too tight. The second jack actually makes it less likely villain has trips.
Raising turns your hand into a bluff. Queens is a showdown value hand here — play it passively.

explanation: This is a nuanced hand strength spot. Queens is an overpair, but the board pairing with a jack is scary. However, think about it logically: the second jack on the turn actually decreases the number of jack combos villain can have (card removal). The button called your preflop raise and a c-bet — they could have many hands that bet the turn as a bluff (missed draws, pocket pairs trying to push you off). Your queens beat all of those. The pot is offering 200-to-80 odds. Folding here is over-folding to turn aggression, which is one of the most common beginner leaks.
keyConceptSummary: A paired board makes trips less likely, not more. Don't over-fold overpairs to single bets on scary-looking turns.
villainRange: BTN turn betting range: Jx, 88, 44, bluffs with draws, pocket pairs

title: Combo Draw — Recognizing Monster Equity
actionHistory: CO raised, you called BTN. BB folded. Flop is 9-8-2 with two hearts. CO bets 35.

options.optionExplanation: Calling is fine but passive. With this much equity, raising is more profitable because you add fold equity to your already strong draw.
Raising is the best play. You have a flush draw and open-ended straight draw — roughly 54% equity. Raising builds the pot and can win it immediately.
Folding a combo draw is a massive error. You have more equity than most made hands here.

explanation: JT of hearts on a 9-8-2 board with two hearts gives you a combo draw: flush draw (9 outs) plus open-ended straight draw (6 clean outs for the straight that aren't hearts). That's approximately 15 outs, giving you around 54% equity — you're actually a favorite against most made hands! This is why understanding hand strength includes understanding drawing equity. A combo draw is often stronger than top pair. Raising here is the best play because you combine your massive equity with fold equity. If villain folds, great. If they call, you're likely ahead anyway.
keyConceptSummary: Combo draws (flush + straight draw) often have more equity than made hands. Play them aggressively.
villainRange: CO betting range: overpairs, top pair, middle pair, air

title: Top Pair Weak Kicker — Proceed with Caution
actionHistory: CO raised, you called from BB. Flop is K-9-4 rainbow. Action on you.

options.optionExplanation: Check-calling is the correct line. You have top pair but a weak kicker, so you want to keep the pot small and see a showdown cheaply.
Donk betting with top pair weak kicker is problematic. Better Kx hands will raise you and you'll be unsure where you stand.
A large lead bet with a marginal hand builds a pot you don't want to play in.

explanation: K-6 on a K-9-4 board gives you top pair but with a terrible kicker. This is a quintessential marginal hand. You beat bluffs and lower pairs, but you lose to any better king (AK, KQ, KJ, KT). The correct strategy with marginal hands is to keep the pot small. Check-calling allows you to see the turn and river at a controlled price. If villain bets multiple streets, you may need to fold. Leading out by betting is a mistake because it builds a pot where you're often behind when called. Understanding the difference between top pair good kicker and top pair weak kicker is a major skill level indicator.
keyConceptSummary: Top pair with a weak kicker is a marginal hand. Keep the pot small by check-calling rather than leading.
villainRange: CO raising and c-bet range: AK, KQ, KJ, Kx, overpairs, broadways, bluffs

title: Pocket Jacks Preflop — The Trickiest Hand
actionHistory: MP raises to 25. Folds to you on the BTN.

options.optionExplanation: Calling is playable but passive. You miss the chance to define villain's range and build the pot with a premium hand in position.
3-betting to 75 is the best play. You have a strong hand in position. 3-betting builds the pot, gets value from worse hands, and narrows villain's range.
Folding pocket jacks on the button is absurdly tight. This is a premium hand that crushes villain's opening range.

explanation: Pocket jacks is the fourth-best starting hand in Hold'em, but beginners often play it too passively because they're afraid of overcards. On the button against a middle position open, JJ should almost always be 3-bet. Here's why: you have a strong hand with position. 3-betting accomplishes several things — it builds the pot with a hand that's ahead of MP's opening range, it narrows their range so you have better information postflop, and it denies the blinds a cheap look. The key is that JJ has roughly 57% equity against a typical MP opening range. That's strong enough to 3-bet for value.
keyConceptSummary: Pocket jacks in position should be 3-bet, not flat called. Don't let fear of overcards make you play premium hands passively.
villainRange: MP open range: 22+, ATo+, A2s+, KTo+, K9s+, QTo+, Q9s+, JTs, T9s

title: River Value Bet with Two Pair
actionHistory: You raised BTN preflop, BB called. You c-bet flop J-7-3, BB called. Turn 2 checked through. River Ace. BB checks.

options.optionExplanation: Checking is too passive. You have two pair and villain has shown weakness. Many hands will call a value bet.
Betting about half pot for value is perfect. Worse jacks, sevens, and even ace-high hands will call. Two pair is comfortably ahead of villain's calling range.
Full pot is too large as a value bet. It folds out many hands that would call a smaller bet.

explanation: The river ace improved your hand from top pair to two pair. This is a clear value betting spot. Villain called the flop and checked the turn and river — their range includes Jx hands, small pairs, maybe 7x, and possibly ace-high hands that picked up a pair on the river. All of these will often call a half-pot bet. The key concept: when you improve on the river and your opponent has shown weakness, bet for value. Don't leave money on the table by checking back hands that are clearly ahead of your opponent's check-calling range.
keyConceptSummary: When you improve to a strong hand on the river and villain checks, bet for value. Don't check back winners.
villainRange: BB passive check range: Jx, 7x, pocket pairs, Ax

title: Nut Flush on the River — Sizing for Maximum Value
actionHistory: You called a BTN raise from CO preflop. Flop K-9-3 two hearts, you called a bet. Turn 7, checked through. River Qh completes your flush. You act first.

options.optionExplanation: Half pot is a reasonable value bet but leaves money on the table. With the nuts, you can size bigger.
A roughly 75-80% pot bet maximizes value. Hands like KQ (just made two pair), Kx with a heart, and flushes will call a large bet.
Check-raising is creative but risky. If villain checks back, you miss value with the nuts. Just bet.

explanation: With the nut flush on the river, your only goal is to extract maximum value. The queen on the river is a perfect card — anyone with KQ just made two pair and will be eager to call or raise. Players with a king and a heart have a worse flush. Even one-pair hands may pay off a sizeable bet. The lesson: when you have the nuts, bet big. Don't bet small hoping for a call — bet the largest amount your opponent's range will call. With a pot of 200, betting 160 targets the top of villain's calling range and maximizes expected value.
keyConceptSummary: With the nuts on the river, size your bet to maximize value from your opponent's calling range. Bet big when you can't lose.
villainRange: BTN range after river Q completing flush: KQ, Kx, Qx, flushes, two pair

title: Recognizing When Your Hand is No Good
actionHistory: You raised UTG, BTN called. You c-bet flop 9-5-2, BTN called. Turn K. You checked, BTN bets 75.

options.optionExplanation: Folding is correct. The king on the turn is terrible for your hand, and BTN's bet after calling the flop strongly suggests they have a king or better.
Calling is hero-calling without a strong reason. The king hits BTN's calling range hard and your overpair is no longer an overpair.
Raising as a bluff with a pair of tens that's likely behind is lighting money on fire.

explanation: This is one of the hardest lessons in poker: knowing when your good hand is no longer good enough. You had an overpair on the flop, but the king on the turn changes everything. The button called your c-bet (suggesting a real hand, not air) and now bets the turn when a king hits. Think about what they call the flop with: pocket pairs, Kx suited hands, suited connectors. The king turn improves KQ, KJ, KTs, K9s, AK — all of which are in their range. Your tens are now losing to all of those. The ability to fold a decent hand when the board changes in your opponent's favor is what separates winning players from losing ones.
keyConceptSummary: When the board changes to favor your opponent's range, good hands become bad hands. Have the discipline to fold.
villainRange: BTN turn betting range on K turn: Kx, 99, AK, KQ, KJ

title: Gutshot Straight Draw — Fold or Continue?
actionHistory: CO raised, you called from BTN. Flop is T-8-3 rainbow. CO bets 25.

options.optionExplanation: Calling is correct. You have a gutshot straight draw (4 outs to the nine), two overcards, and good position. The price is right to see a turn.
Folding with two overcards, a gutshot, and position at a good price is too tight. You have plenty of equity to continue.
Raising with just a gutshot and overcards is too aggressive at this point. Your hand has showdown potential but not enough equity for a raise.

explanation: QJ on a T-8-3 board gives you a gutshot straight draw — you need a nine to make the nut straight. That's only 4 outs, but you also have two overcards (queen and jack) that could pair for top pair. Altogether you have roughly 10 outs, which is about 20% equity on the flop. You're being asked to call 25 into a 75 pot (25 to win 50), needing about 33% — but with implied odds (how much more you'll win when you hit), this is an easy call with position. The lesson: gutshot draws alone are marginal, but when combined with overcards and position, they become good continuing hands.
keyConceptSummary: Gutshot draws with overcards and position are worth calling small bets. Count all your outs, not just the draw.
villainRange: CO c-bet range: overpairs, top pair, middle pair, broadways, air

title: Full House — Don't Slow Play on Wet Boards
actionHistory: You raised BTN, BB called. You bet flop 9-8-7 with two hearts, BB called. Turn pairs the 8 giving you a full house. BB checks.

options.optionExplanation: Betting for value is correct. The board is extremely wet and villain likely has a straight, flush draw, or trips that will pay you off.
Slow-playing a full house on this dangerous board is a mistake. Villain has tons of draws and made hands that will pay you now.
Full pot is playable but slightly too large. You want to size to get max calls from straights and flush draws.

explanation: You have nines full of eights — a monster hand. But the board is incredibly dangerous: 9-8-7-8 with two hearts. Many hands in villain's range have strong draws or made straights (JT, T6, 65). If you check, a heart river could complete a flush and kill your action, or villain might check behind with a hand that would have called. With a full house on a wet board, you must bet to extract value from the many hands that are currently drawing dead or are committed to calling. This is the opposite of the set-on-a-dry-board scenario — on wet boards, monster hands should be played fast.
keyConceptSummary: On wet, dangerous boards, play monster hands fast. Don't slow-play when the board could kill your action on future streets.
villainRange: BB calling range: straights (JT, T6), flush draws, trips, two pair, overpairs

title: Trips vs. Sets — Understanding the Difference
actionHistory: You raised from CO, BB called. Flop is A-7-2 rainbow. BB checks.

options.optionExplanation: A small value bet is perfect. You have a set — one of the strongest hands possible. Villain will often have an ace and pay you off.
Checking is too slow. With an ace on board, villain likely has a pair of aces and will call bets on every street.
Full pot is a bit large. You want to keep villain's full range calling, not just strong aces.

explanation: You flopped a set of sevens — this is different from trips. A set means you have a pocket pair and one matching card on the board. Trips means two cards on the board match one in your hand. Sets are much stronger because they're disguised — your opponent can't easily put you on three sevens when they see A-7-2 on the board. The ace on the flop is actually great for you because villain will often have Ax and refuse to fold. Bet small for value, let them hang themselves. Over three streets of betting, you'll win a big pot while they think their ace is good.
keyConceptSummary: Sets are powerful and disguised. When your opponent likely has a pair they love (like top pair with an ace), bet small and extract value steadily.
villainRange: BB defending range: Ax, pocket pairs, suited connectors, suited aces
